explicit_text: I moved to Vancouver in 1935 with my family, settling first in the Dunbar area. Vancouver was the site of intense labor conflicts, including the 1935 waterfront strike where I witnessed machine guns on the docks. Later in life, I returned to Vancouver for the 2014 Pacific Coast Pensioners Association convention and shared my experiences as a longshoreman and Navy veteran.

explicit_text: I was born in Victoria, British Columbia, in 1926, and spent much of my early life in this province. British Columbia's waterfront was a hotbed of labor activity, especially during the 1935 strike, which deeply affected my family. I also trained and served in the Canadian Navy, with time spent in Esquimalt, British Columbia, during the Second World War.

explicit_text: In 2014, I participated in the Pacific Coast Pensioners Association convention in Vancouver, where I gave an oral history interview about my life and work. The association represents retired longshore workers like myself, and it was important to share stories about strikes, pensions, and labor struggles. Being part of the PCPA helped me connect with others who lived through similar experiences on the waterfront.

explicit_text: I was a member of the International Longshore and Warehouse Union Local 500, and I walked the picket lines during the 1958 ILWU strike. The union was central to my working life on the waterfront, and I fought to have blacklisted workers from the 1935 strike reinstated in 1966. The ILWU shaped much of my career and my identity as a longshoreman in British Columbia.

explicit_text: My father grew up on a wheat farm in Saskatchewan before going overseas in the First World War. After the war, he worked as a postmaster in Insinger, Saskatchewan, before joining the Naval Reserve and moving west. Saskatchewan was where my father’s early life and work began before he settled in British Columbia.

explicit_text: The 1935 waterfront strike in British Columbia was a defining event in my childhood. My father was a scab during that strike, which caused tension and even violence on the docks, including machine guns being present. The strike left a lasting impact on my family and the labor community, with blacklisting affecting many workers for years.

explicit_text: In 1966, I was involved in efforts to get blacklisted workers from the 1935 strike reinstated, including those who had scabbed. This Victoria Day strike was part of ongoing labor struggles on the waterfront, and I fought to have these men’s time recognized for pensions. It was a moment of reconciliation and progress within the union community.

explicit_text: I joined the Canadian Navy in 1942 when I was just 15 years and 8 months old, having lied about my age to get in. I trained in Esquimalt, British Columbia, and served on a minesweeper escorting convoys between Newfoundland, Halifax, and the eastern U.S. coast during the Second World War. My Navy service was a formative experience that took me across Canada and into American ports like Baltimore and New York City.

explicit_text: Early in my Navy career, I was stationed at Prince Rupert, British Columbia, where I spent about six months before applying for a gunner’s course. Prince Rupert was my first posting, and it was there I began to understand naval life and my role in the war effort. It was a stepping stone before moving to Esquimalt for further training.

explicit_text: During my naval service in the Second World War, I was part of escort convoys that traveled to Newfoundland and Labrador as part of the triangle run. These voyages were critical for protecting shipping from enemy submarines along the Atlantic coast. Newfoundland and Labrador were important stops on our route between Canada and the eastern United States.

explicit_text: In September 1943, our minesweeper went into Curtis Bay, a suburb of Baltimore, for a refit. These stops gave me a chance to experience life in the U.S. during the war and connect with American sailors.
